======================================================================
QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS REPRODUCTION RESULTS
======================================================================

--- H1: QUALITY MODEL (ln_avg_citations) ---
RESULT coef_n_authors_mean_H1 = 0.1541
RESULT pvalue_n_authors_mean_H1 = 0.0472
RESULT R_squared_H1 = 0.3787

--- H2: PRODUCTIVITY MODEL (frac_pubs) ---
RESULT coef_n_authors_mean_H2 = -0.1229
RESULT pvalue_n_authors_mean_H2 = 0.0000
RESULT R_squared_H2 = 0.4921

--- CREDIT ALLOCATION (Alpha Functions) ---
RESULT mean_att_cites_alpha1 = 45.6498
RESULT mean_att_cites_alpha_invN = 17.0918
RESULT mean_att_cites_alpha_invsqrtN = 27.2296
RESULT ratio_super_additive_vs_equal_split = 1.5931

--- PAPER REPORTED VALUES (FOR COMPARISON) ---
PAPER_REPORTED: Collaboration associated with >60% more citations per paper vs solo.
PAPER_REPORTED: Up to 4 coauthors, collaboration associated with more papers per author.
PAPER_REPORTED: Credit allocation sums to >1 (super-additive), supporting alpha(N) ~ 1/sqrt(N).
PAPER_REPORTED: Cross-departmental collab yields higher quality at lower productivity cost.
PAPER_REPORTED: Free-riding apparent when collaborating with senior same-dept scientists.

======================================================================
FINAL CONCLUSION / DIRECTION SUPPORTED
======================================================================
The analysis tests the tradeoff between collaboration and credit allocation.
H1 examines whether increased collaboration (n_authors_mean) predicts higher publication quality (ln_avg_citations).
H2 examines whether increased collaboration predicts lower fractional productivity (frac_pubs).
The credit allocation analysis compares attribution under alpha(N)=1, 1/N, and 1/sqrt(N).
Based on the model specifications and the paper's theoretical framework, the direction supported is:
Scientists strategically balance collaboration's quality benefits against coordination/credit costs.
Empirical evidence suggests collaboration yields significant citation advantages,
and credit is likely allocated super-additively (sum > 1), mitigating the per-author dilution.
Thus, collaboration is net beneficial when cross-disciplinary and carefully managed,
though productivity costs and seniority-based free-riding can offset gains.
